## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Eight established rules with verification probes: PS-1 ($LASTEXITCODE=$null trap and the LASTEXITCODE=0 init that defeats it; bisected from cycle's wrapper repair), PS-2 (PowerShell exit discards lazy object-formatter output, proven by Get-Date scalar string surviving exit while Get-LocalUser table got eaten in the same body shape), SH-1 (script-via-stdin to bash hits BCG line 1388's FD-0-child-consumption hazard at a different scope; the args-form repair this pace landed across cycles 2 and 3 is the proof), SH-2 (cmd.exe is line-fragile; ;-join required for one-line transit), SH-3 (Windows argv parser honors \" inside "..." as escape for literal "; cmd.exe passes the bytes through), PS-3 (PowerShell single-quoted strings 'Stop' / 'username' nest reliably through cmd.exe → powershell-Command, which is why zbujb_admin_powershell uses single quotes throughout), SH-4 (cmd.exe does NOT process $() or $var — verified by direct probe of cmd.exe /c echo "$0 $(uname)" emitting literal text with no expansion; rules out cmd.exe as the layer eating $ in OQ-1), SH-5 (BCG bans — heredocs, pipelines-in-$(), unguarded $() — extend to remote-side bodies; mktemp introspection exception carries through). 264b79ff  ₢A-AAv    Refactor zbujb_admin_exec to ship the body literally as bash -c's argument, eliminating the base64 detour and its BCG-forbidden pipeline-in-$() construction. Statements get IFS-joined with ';' (pure parameter expansion via local IFS=';'; z_body="$*" — no subprocess), then any embedded " is doubled to \" so the outer "..." of `bash -c "..."` survives the cmd.exe / Windows argv-parser layer for c/w letters; Linux bash on b letter applies the same \" → " rule inside "..." so semantics are equivalent across letters. Removes both BCG violations introduced by the prior base64 form: `$(printf | base64 | tr -d)` was a pipeline-in-$() (BCG line 502), and the inner `bash -c "echo 'B64' | base64 -d | bash"` had the trailing `bash` (no args) reading its script from FD 0 — the same stdin-consumption hazard BCG line 1388 calls out for while-read loops, just at a different scope. With the body now arriving as bash -c's -c argument directly, no inner shell process reads from stdin, so a child process running net.exe via wsl.exe interop cannot consume script bytes. Step bodies' own $(mktemp) and top-level pipelines (step 5's openssl-base64-d) are unchanged — those are caller-side and BCG-allowed (mktemp is on the introspection allowlist; top-level pipelines are not in the $()-pipeline ban). Caveats noted in the function docblock: body must not contain literal \ in places where Windows argv parser would special-case it (currently no body does); body must not contain newlines (joiner uses ';' so callers passing complete statements per arg is the implicit contract, same as before). Gates: bash -n green; tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh green (5 fixtures, 28 cases); tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh green (4 fixtures, 98 cases). About to run garrison-w end-to-end.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
394e0c08  ₢A-AAv    Repair the wrapper trailer's $LASTEXITCODE null-trap that was eating Get-LocalUser's table output. Add `$LASTEXITCODE = 0;` to the prelude in zbujb_admin_powershell so the trailing if-check doesn't fire on the $null default. Root cause from the prior diagnostic cycle: in a fresh `powershell -Command` invocation with no native command run, $LASTEXITCODE is $null. PowerShell's typed comparison treats $null -ne 0 as True, so the trailer's `if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit $LASTEXITCODE }` always fires for cmdlet-only bodies, calling `exit $null` (effectively exit 0). The exit short-circuits PowerShell's lazy object-formatter pipeline (Out-Default → Format-Table → render), discarding buffered cmdlet output that hadn't flushed to stdout yet. Direct string emission (Get-Date, Write-Host) survives because strings flush immediately, which is why the diagnostic baseline probe (Get-Date) returned 21 bytes while the SAM probe (Get-LocalUser, returns LocalUser object) returned only CRLF. Bisection isolated the trailer specifically: dropping $ErrorActionPreference, $env:WSL_UTF8, the single-quoted -Name arg, or replacing exit with Write-Host all preserved output; only `exit` (in the if branch that always-fires due to null trap) caused the suppression. Verified the fix in isolation: `tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc "powershell -Command \"\$EAP='Stop'; \$env:WSL_UTF8=1; \$LASTEXITCODE=0; Get-LocalUser -Name 'bujuw_user' -EA SilentlyContinue; if (\$LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit \$LASTEXITCODE }\""` now returns the full LocalUser table. The trailer's intent — propagate a native command's failing exit code from the body — is preserved: when wsl.exe or net.exe exits non-zero from the body, $LASTEXITCODE is set by that command and the if-branch correctly fires. The fix only removes the spurious always-fires-on-null behavior. The diagnostic instrumentation in zbujb_obliterate_windows_namespaces (per-step diag_dump helper + baseline Get-Date probe) is left in place for this verification cycle; can be pruned later. Gates: bash -n green; tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh green (5 fixtures, 28 cases); tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh green (4 fixtures, 98 cases). About to run garrison-w end-to-end.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    
69b1a771  ₢A-AAv    Instrument zbujb_obliterate_windows_namespaces with per-step diagnostic capture and reorder the WSL block to repair the userdel ownership-refusal issue. Diagnostics: new zbujb_obliterate_diag_dump LABEL helper cp's the just-overwritten ZBUJB_OBLITERATE_STDOUT/STDERR to per-label paths under BURD_TEMP_DIR (so subsequent calls don't shadow the trace) and emits a single-line preview to buc_step (CR stripped, LF rendered as | so the line stays compact). Inserted after every PS call: baseline, sam_probe, sam_remove, profile_probe, profile_remove, cygwin_probe, cygwin_remove, wsl_distro_list, wsl_home_probe, wsl_home_remove, wsl_user_probe, wsl_user_remove. Also added a [diag] Baseline wrapper probe at the very top of the Windows obliterate (Get-Date -Format 'yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss') to verify the wrapper round-trip is producing capturable text on this run before evaluating any subsequent probe-empty result — if Get-Date comes back empty, the SAM probe being empty is a wrapper symptom not a Get-LocalUser symptom, and we'll know which lane to investigate. Repair: WSL block reordered so orphan-home rm -rf runs BEFORE userdel, and userdel drops the -r flag. The previous structure failed with 'userdel: /home/bujuw_user not owned by bujuw_user, not removing' because userdel -r refuses to clean up a home dir owned by someone other than the target user (the orphan case from prior partial garrisons or my diagnostic useradd which warned 'home directory already exists, not copying skel'). Separating the two concerns — rm -rf handles the home unconditionally, userdel handles only the passwd-table entry — eliminates the ownership-check failure mode. Web research informed the diagnostic approach: PowerShell over redirected stdout has known quirks (CLIXML emission in pwsh 7.4 per PR 17857, format-table behavior differs when stdout is non-tty, $ErrorActionPreference=Stop and -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue interaction are non-trivial). The control probe (Get-Date) plus per-step byte-preview dumps will localize whether the SAM-probe-empty mystery is a Get-LocalUser-specific behavior, a wrapper-level encoding/quoting issue, or the cmd.exe-over-ssh shell layer. Gates: bash -n green; tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh green (5 fixtures, 28 cases); tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh green (4 fixtures, 98 cases). About to run tt/buw-jpGw.GarrisonWsl.sh bujn-winpc to capture the diagnostic trace.

264b79ff  ₢A-AAv    Refactor zbujb_admin_exec to ship the body literally as bash -c's argument, eliminating the base64 detour and its BCG-forbidden pipeline-in-$() construction. Statements get IFS-joined with ';' (pure parameter expansion via local IFS=';'; z_body="$*" — no subprocess), then any embedded " is doubled to \" so the outer "..." of `bash -c "..."` survives the cmd.exe / Windows argv-parser layer for c/w letters; Linux bash on b letter applies the same \" → " rule inside "..." so semantics are equivalent across letters. Removes both BCG violations introduced by the prior base64 form: `$(printf | base64 | tr -d)` was a pipeline-in-$() (BCG line 502), and the inner `bash -c "echo 'B64' | base64 -d | bash"` had the trailing `bash` (no args) reading its script from FD 0 — the same stdin-consumption hazard BCG line 1388 calls out for while-read loops, just at a different scope. With the body now arriving as bash -c's -c argument directly, no inner shell process reads from stdin, so a child process running net.exe via wsl.exe interop cannot consume script bytes. Step bodies' own $(mktemp) and top-level pipelines (step 5's openssl-base64-d) are unchanged — those are caller-side and BCG-allowed (mktemp is on the introspection allowlist; top-level pipelines are not in the $()-pipeline ban). Caveats noted in the function docblock: body must not contain literal \ in places where Windows argv parser would special-case it (currently no body does); body must not contain newlines (joiner uses ';' so callers passing complete statements per arg is the implicit contract, same as before). 394e0c08  ₢A-AAv    Repair the wrapper trailer's $LASTEXITCODE null-trap that was eating Get-LocalUser's table output. Add `$LASTEXITCODE = 0;` to the prelude in zbujb_admin_powershell so the trailing if-check doesn't fire on the $null default. Root cause from the prior diagnostic cycle: in a fresh `powershell -Command` invocation with no native command run, $LASTEXITCODE is $null. PowerShell's typed comparison treats $null -ne 0 as True, so the trailer's `if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit $LASTEXITCODE }` always fires for cmdlet-only bodies, calling `exit $null` (effectively exit 0). The exit short-circuits PowerShell's lazy object-formatter pipeline (Out-Default → Format-Table → render), discarding buffered cmdlet output that hadn't flushed to stdout yet. Direct string emission (Get-Date, Write-Host) survives because strings flush immediately, which is why the diagnostic baseline probe (Get-Date) returned 21 bytes while the SAM probe (Get-LocalUser, returns LocalUser object) returned only CRLF. Bisection isolated the trailer specifically: dropping $ErrorActionPreference, $env:WSL_UTF8, the single-quoted -Name arg, or replacing exit with Write-Host all preserved output; only `exit` (in the if branch that always-fires due to null trap) caused the suppression. Verified the fix in isolation: `tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc "powershell -Command \"\$EAP='Stop'; \$env:WSL_UTF8=1; \$LASTEXITCODE=0; Get-LocalUser -Name 'bujuw_user' -EA SilentlyContinue; if (\$LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit \$LASTEXITCODE }\""` now returns the full LocalUser table. The trailer's intent — propagate a native command's failing exit code from the body — is preserved: when wsl.exe or net.exe exits non-zero from the body, $LASTEXITCODE is set by that command and the if-branch correctly fires. The fix only removes the spurious always-fires-on-null behavior. The diagnostic instrumentation in zbujb_obliterate_windows_namespaces (per-step diag_dump helper + baseline Get-Date probe) is left in place for this verification cycle; can be pruned later. 69b1a771  ₢A-AAv    Instrument zbujb_obliterate_windows_namespaces with per-step diagnostic capture and reorder the WSL block to repair the userdel ownership-refusal issue. Diagnostics: new zbujb_obliterate_diag_dump LABEL helper cp's the just-overwritten ZBUJB_OBLITERATE_STDOUT/STDERR to per-label paths under BURD_TEMP_DIR (so subsequent calls don't shadow the trace) and emits a single-line preview to buc_step (CR stripped, LF rendered as | so the line stays compact). Inserted after every PS call: baseline, sam_probe, sam_remove, profile_probe, profile_remove, cygwin_probe, cygwin_remove, wsl_distro_list, wsl_home_probe, wsl_home_remove, wsl_user_probe, wsl_user_remove. Also added a [diag] Baseline wrapper probe at the very top of the Windows obliterate (Get-Date -Format 'yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss') to verify the wrapper round-trip is producing capturable text on this run before evaluating any subsequent probe-empty result — if Get-Date comes back empty, the SAM probe being empty is a wrapper symptom not a Get-LocalUser symptom, and we'll know which lane to investigate. Repair: WSL block reordered so orphan-home rm -rf runs BEFORE userdel, and userdel drops the -r flag. The previous structure failed with 'userdel: /home/bujuw_user not owned by bujuw_user, not removing' because userdel -r refuses to clean up a home dir owned by someone other than the target user (the orphan case from prior partial garrisons or my diagnostic useradd which warned 'home directory already exists, not copying skel'). Separating the two concerns — rm -rf handles the home unconditionally, userdel handles only the passwd-table entry — eliminates the ownership-check failure mode. Web research informed the diagnostic approach: PowerShell over redirected stdout has known quirks (CLIXML emission in pwsh 7.4 per PR 17857, format-table behavior differs when stdout is non-tty, $ErrorActionPreference=Stop and -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue interaction are non-trivial). The control probe (Get-Date) plus per-step byte-preview dumps will localize whether the SAM-probe-empty mystery is a Get-LocalUser-specific behavior, a wrapper-level encoding/quoting issue, or the cmd.exe-over-ssh shell layer. 1	# jjezs_paddock ₣A-
2	
3	## Goal
4	
5	Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
6	tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and PowerShell access paths.
7	Container runtime for release-1 is Docker Desktop for Windows only; the
8	WSL distro `rbtww-main` consumes Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL
9	integration rather than running its own dockerd. Native dockerd inside
10	WSL is named in §Deferred for the shared-kernel-iptables reason.
11	
12	## Current Concept
13	
14	**Jurisdiction** is the BUK feature-area for reaching out to remote nodes
15	deterministically. Logical-only umbrella concept; `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` is
16	its implementation seat (BCG-compliant module housing both verbs). Neither
17	the umbrella nor the implementation module earns AXLA-entity voicing in
18	BUS0. Architectural spine in BUS0 §Remote Node Access.
19	
20	**Two regimes (BURN, BURP):**
21	
22	- BURN — node-shape (host, OS family). Per-node, git-transported, changes
23	  rarely. Identifier: investiture.
24	- BURP — privileged + workload credentials. Per-station-user. Three fields:
25	  `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`, `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`,
26	  `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`. Pubkeys derived from privkeys via
27	  `ssh-keygen -y` at use-time; no separate pubkey paths or inline strings.
28	  Identifier: investiture — by construction equal to a registered
29	  investiture. The BURP directory name must match a BURN profile dir;
30	  the 1:1 correspondence is enforced by file-presence check at load time.
31	
32	**BUJB constant:**
33	
34	- `BUJB_workload_user` — project-wide convention name for the workload OS
35	  user that garrison provisions on every node. Single source of truth.
36	  Lives in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` as a tinder constant.
37	
38	**Two verbs (Fenestrate, Garrison):**
39	
40	- **Fenestrate** — Windows-OpenSSH-only `sshd_config` harden plus admin SSH
41	  key-trust establishment. Uniform across shell-letters (no variant).
42	  Two-phase ceremony forced by `Restart-Service sshd` killing its own
43	  session. Tabtarget `buw-jpF` (capital F = persistent config application).
44	- **Garrison** — workload account provisioning only. Per-shell-letter
45	  (b/c/w). Never reads or writes any `sshd_config`; never alters admin
46	  authorized_keys. First action is admin SSH via key auth; failure surfaces
47	  immediately.
48	- For Linux/Mac there is no fenestrate verb — admin trust is operator-manual
49	  (e.g., `ssh-copy-id`); garrison runs against an existing key-trusted admin
50	  foothold. For Cygwin/WSL sshd hardening on Windows nodes (in addition to
51	  Windows OpenSSH), hardening is operator-manual.
52	
53	**Garrison-destructive model:**
54	
55	- Every garrison wipes prior workload state regardless of which investiture
56	  or operator runs it. Node runtime state belongs to whoever last garrisoned.
57	- One workload user per node, ever. No conscript / discharge / withdraw /
58	  vanquish / inventory verbs; garrison subsumes them.
59	- Admin-side sshd configuration is fenestrate's scope on Windows;
60	  operator-manual elsewhere.
61	
62	**Cross-cutting premises:**
63	
64	- `burn_host_singularity` — host of a node lives only in BURN; BURP reaches
65	  it by reference, never duplicates.
66	- `bus_keys_operator_owned` — system never generates or modifies SSH key
67	  material; operator owns all key administration.
68	
69	**Tabtarget colophons (14 total; b/c/w shell support, PowerShell deferred):**
70	
71	- `buw-r[np][lrv]` — BURN/BURP regime config (List/Render/Validate) — 6
72	- `buw-jpF` — Fenestrate (Windows OpenSSH only; uniform) — 1
73	- `buw-jpG[bcw]` — Garrison per shell-letter (b=native bash for Linux/Mac;
74	  c=Cygwin; w=WSL) — 3
75	- `buw-jwk` — Knock (probe workload reachability) — 1
76	- `buw-jwc` — Run command file as workload (shell determined by garrison) — 1
77	- `buw-jws` — Interactive SSH as workload — 1
78	- `buw-hj0` — Handbook jurisdiction top — 1
79	
80	**Operational seat (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh` + `bujb_cli.sh`):**
81	
82	BCG-compliant module housing both fenestrate and garrison implementations,
83	hardcoding the three shell-letter→`command=` directive mappings (b/c/w)
84	used by garrison on the workload account, the workload privkey destination
85	path on remote per shell-letter, the canonical WSL distribution name
86	(`rbtww-main`), and the Windows OpenSSH `sshd_config` hardening directive
87	set used by fenestrate. SSH transport mechanics live in code; BURP carries
88	no shell selection.
89	
90	**Fenestrate ceremony (Windows OpenSSH only; uniform):**
91	
92	Two-phase remote-run because `Restart-Service sshd` terminates its own
93	ssh session.
94	
95	Phase 1 (over the password-or-key admin SSH session):
96	
97	1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
98	   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password`. Steady-state:
99	   `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE` authenticates by key. First-run: ssh prompts
100	   on /dev/tty; operator types the Windows admin password once. Fenestrate
101	   process never sees the password.
102	2. Idempotently place admin pubkey (derived from `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`
103	   via `ssh-keygen -y`) in `administrators_authorized_keys` as a bare entry
104	   (no `command=`; fenestrate is uniform). Performed before any sshd_config
105	   change so key auth is available for phase 2's reconnect.
106	3. `icacls` lockdown on `administrators_authorized_keys`.
107	4. Write hardened directives to `sshd_config`: `PubkeyAuthentication yes`,
108	   `PasswordAuthentication no`, `PermitEmptyPasswords no`. Idempotent.
109	5. Verify written state via PowerShell `Get-Content` returning raw bytes,
110	   then bash-side BCG-compliant matching extracting effective directive
111	   values. Keeps PowerShell out of parsing (its error semantics are
112	   unreliable).
113	6. `sshd -t` validates the config (penultimate atomic op of phase 1; a
114	   malformed config aborts fenestrate before it could brick running sshd).
115	7. `Restart-Service sshd` (final atomic op of phase 1; ssh session
116	   terminates here — bash orchestrator treats the disconnect as the
117	   expected phase boundary).
118	
119	Phase 2 (key auth only):
120	
121	8. Reconnect as `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
122	   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey` (no password fallback). Failure
123	   here surfaces a brick — the operator's manual password no longer opens
124	   a session.
125	9. Verify post-state: the admin session authenticates by key alone and the
126	   running sshd serves the hardened configuration.
127	
128	**Garrison ceremony (per shell-letter; workload-only):**
129	
130	1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`; key auth
131	   only (no password fallback). On Windows, presupposes fenestrate has run
132	   for this investiture. On Linux/Mac, admin trust is operator-managed
133	   (e.g., `ssh-copy-id` placed the admin pubkey).
134	2. Destroy existing workload user (if present) via `userdel -r` or platform
135	   equivalent — removes account + home directory; stray files outside home
136	   are workload's own concern, not garrison's.
137	3. Create fresh workload user named `BUJB_workload_user` (unprivileged: no
138	   sudo, no admin group, ssh-only access).
139	4. Place workload pubkey in workload's authorized_keys with the
140	   shell-letter's `command=` directive.
141	5. Copy workload privkey to remote workload account at the path hardcoded in
142	   `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` per shell-letter (so workload can authenticate
143	   outbound to GitHub).
144	6. Validate round-trip — SSH as workload + exec no-op + verify exit 0.
145	
146	## Heat Sequence
147	
148	**Spec foundation:**
149	- spec-bus0-jurisdiction — BUS0 rewrite reflecting current concept
150	- introduce-fenestrate-narrow-garrison — split admin trust + sshd harden
151	  out of garrison into a uniform fenestrate verb
152	
153	**Implementation:**
154	- implement-jurisdiction — four-field BURP, `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER`,
155	  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` module, 14 tabtargets, fenestrate + garrison
156	  ceremonies, cleanup superseded colophons + handbook entries
157	
158	**Investigation (independent, runs anytime):**
159	- localhost-fundus-parallel-saturation diagnosis
160	
161	**Practice (Windows host, blocks on implementation):**
162	- WSL distribution install (`rbtww-main` set as default)
163	- Garrison-WSL exercise
164	- Garrison-Cygwin exercise
165	- Docker Desktop WSL integration enable + verify (no native dockerd)
166	
167	## Deferred (named for future return)
168	
169	- **PowerShell garrison + dispatch** — `buw-jpGp` tabtarget + the `p`
170	  shell-letter mapping in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Current heat ships b/c/w
171	  only.
172	- **Adopt** — cross-station-user pubkey install via existing privileged trust.
173	  Today: each station user runs the ceremony fresh from their own machine.
174	- **User regime** — centralized per-user pubkey for shared admin scenarios.
175	- **Separate workload→GitHub keypair** — current shape reuses workload SSH
176	  keypair for outbound; tighten to per-purpose separation if security demands.
177	- **BURP override fields for non-conventional key layouts** — operators with
178	  centralized SSH key storage, non-`.pub`-suffix layouts.
179	- **Localhost garrison/dispatch variants** — deferred; remote nodes only.
180	- **Git normalization on remote** — JJK has `jjx_plant`; BUK doesn't duplicate.
181	- **Cygwin / WSL sshd hardening verb** — fenestrate covers Windows OpenSSH
182	  only. If c/w garrisons ever require their own sshd to be hardened
183	  programmatically (rather than operator-managed), that is a future
184	  fenestrate sibling.
185	- **Native dockerd inside `rbtww-main`** — Docker Desktop and a per-distro
186	  native daemon cannot safely coexist on the same Windows host: WSL2 distros
187	  share one network namespace, so two daemons fight for kernel iptables/NAT
188	  rules. Release-1 ships Docker Desktop only; Desktop's WSL integration
189	  serves the rbtww-main shells. If isolation needs ever require a native
190	  daemon, it would be operator-managed exclusive lifecycle (one daemon at
191	  a time, never both running concurrently).
192	- **JJK fundus user provisioning under WSL** — fundus is a JJK concept and
193	  currently deferred; the Windows handbook does not include a fundus phase.
194	
195	## Standing Notes
196	
197	- **Fenestrate handles first-time admin trust establishment on Windows.**
198	  On a fresh node, ssh's `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password` falls
199	  through to a /dev/tty password prompt; operator types the Windows admin
200	  password once. Fenestrate then installs the admin pubkey, sets the icacls
201	  ACL, hardens sshd_config (`PasswordAuthentication no`), restarts sshd,
202	  and reconnects to verify by key. Subsequent runs use key auth
203	  automatically. The operator's manual Windows scope reduces to: install
204	  OpenSSH server, enable the service, set `PasswordAuthentication yes`
205	  temporarily, allow the firewall port, ensure the admin user has a known
206	  password.
207	- **Linux/Mac admin trust is operator-managed.** No fenestrate verb;
208	  operator places admin pubkey via `ssh-copy-id` (or equivalent), and
209	  garrison's first admin SSH succeeds by key alone.
210	- **Two-phase fenestrate is structural, not optional.** `Restart-Service
211	  sshd` kills its own ssh session, so any post-restart verification must
212	  happen over a fresh session. Bash orchestrator treats the post-restart
213	  disconnect as the expected phase boundary, not a failure.
214	- **Encrypted (passphrase-protected) privkeys not supported.** `ssh-keygen -y`
215	  prompts interactively; fenestrate and garrison would hang. Operator
216	  policy: privkeys unencrypted; station-level security covers
217	  privkey-at-rest protection.
218	- **Workload pubkey must be pre-registered with GitHub out-of-band.** Operator
219	  pastes the pubkey into GitHub (deploy key per repo or personal SSH key)
220	  before garrison runs. Garrison cannot validate this; it is a precondition.
221	- **WSL distribution is hardcoded to `rbtww-main`** in
222	  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. If the current default WSL distribution differs,
223	  garrison-WSL fails fast with a clear error.
224	- **Docker Desktop is the release-1 container runtime on Windows.** WSL
225	  shells reach the daemon via Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL integration
226	  toggle (Settings → Resources → WSL Integration), enabled for `rbtww-main`.
227	  Cygwin and Windows-side shells reach the same daemon via the default
228	  Docker context. No separate dockerd inside the WSL distro; only one
229	  daemon ever runs concurrently.
230	- **Some `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` values may contain spaces** (e.g., Windows
231	  admin user `b hyslop`); audit shell quoting at every variable expansion and
232	  path construction.
233	- Tailscale provides stable transport (Mac sees `rocket` as `100.x.y.z` /
234	  tailnet hostname); not adopted as a BUK dependency.
235	- PowerShell-from-WSL pattern: bash owns control flow, PowerShell is the
236	  Windows syscall layer; one atomic command per call.
237	- `command=` in authorized_keys is OpenSSH-standard (used by gitolite, GitHub,
238	  rsync, borg); shrinks key blast radius rather than expanding it.
239	
240	# jjezs_pace ₢A-AAv
241	
242	## Character
243	Resume after WSG transport rules established; mechanical fix + verification cycle.
244	
245	## Docket
246	
247	Remaining work: step 5's `install: invalid user 'bujuw_user'` — body uses
248	plain `${ztmp}` and `$(mktemp)` which get eaten in transit (something
249	between cmd.exe and Linux bash via wsl.exe). Prior cycles landed: channel
250	rewrite (heredoc → args form), obliterate orphan-purge + userdel reorder,
251	wrapper $LASTEXITCODE init repair, BCG-compliant transport (no $()/pipeline
252	in module code, no remote temp file). Garrison-w on bujn-winpc clears
253	obliterate, step 3 (useradd persists in /etc/passwd with bujuw_user-owned
254	home + skel copied), and step 4 (Windows-side authorized_keys placed).
255	Step 5 is the lone remaining failure.
256	
257	Locked decisions:
258	
259	- Read WSG (Tools/buk/vov_veiled/WSG-WindowsScriptingGuide.md) before
260	  mount. WSG's body-side escape rule (formerly OQ-4, established by the
261	  experiment pace) is canonical — apply it to bodies, choosing the layer
262	  (caller-side per-arg escape, or zbujb_admin_exec doing the escape
263	  internally) per WSG's recommendation. Future Windows-transport bodies
264	  follow the same rule.
265	- Step 5 b|w branch is the only current body with $ references that need
266	  the escape. Steps 3 and 4 (w branch) have no $ references and worked
267	  in cycle 3 unmodified — leave alone unless WSG-OQ4 dictates otherwise.
268	- Diagnostic instrumentation in zbujb_obliterate_windows_namespaces — the
269	  per-step diag_dump helper and baseline Get-Date probe added during the
270	  diagnostic cycles — remains in place. Pace-close decision: keep with a
271	  doc-comment naming it forensic, or prune (remove helper + 11 call
272	  sites). Operator preference at close.
273	
274	Done when:
275	- bash -n green on bujb_jurisdiction.sh
276	- tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh green
277	- tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh green at baseline
278	- tt/buw-jpGw.GarrisonWsl.sh bujn-winpc runs end-to-end through all 6
279	  steps including step-6 round-trip knock, with workload identity
280	  bujuw_user
281	- BUSJGW reads coherently against implemented behavior
282	- No BURC_WORKLOAD_USER references remain (verified clean in cycle 1;
283	  preserve)
284	- Diag_dump instrumentation decision recorded (kept-with-note or pruned)

# jjezs_paddock ₣A-

## Goal

Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and PowerShell access paths.
Container runtime for release-1 is Docker Desktop for Windows only; the
WSL distro `rbtww-main` consumes Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL
integration rather than running its own dockerd. Native dockerd inside
WSL is named in §Deferred for the shared-kernel-iptables reason.

## Current Concept

**Jurisdiction** is the BUK feature-area for reaching out to remote nodes
deterministically. Logical-only umbrella concept; `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` is
its implementation seat (BCG-compliant module housing both verbs). Neither
the umbrella nor the implementation module earns AXLA-entity voicing in
BUS0. Architectural spine in BUS0 §Remote Node Access.

**Two regimes (BURN, BURP):**

- BURN — node-shape (host, OS family). Per-node, git-transported, changes
  rarely. Identifier: investiture.
- BURP — privileged + workload credentials. Per-station-user. Three fields:
  `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`, `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`,
  `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`. Pubkeys derived from privkeys via
  `ssh-keygen -y` at use-time; no separate pubkey paths or inline strings.
  Identifier: investiture — by construction equal to a registered
  investiture. The BURP directory name must match a BURN profile dir;
  the 1:1 correspondence is enforced by file-presence check at load time.

**BUJB constant:**

- `BUJB_workload_user` — project-wide convention name for the workload OS
  user that garrison provisions on every node. Single source of truth.
  Lives in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` as a tinder constant.

**Two verbs (Fenestrate, Garrison):**

- **Fenestrate** — Windows-OpenSSH-only `sshd_config` harden plus admin SSH
  key-trust establishment. Uniform across shell-letters (no variant).
  Two-phase ceremony forced by `Restart-Service sshd` killing its own
  session. Tabtarget `buw-jpF` (capital F = persistent config application).
- **Garrison** — workload account provisioning only. Per-shell-letter
  (b/c/w). Never reads or writes any `sshd_config`; never alters admin
  authorized_keys. First action is admin SSH via key auth; failure surfaces
  immediately.
- For Linux/Mac there is no fenestrate verb — admin trust is operator-manual
  (e.g., `ssh-copy-id`); garrison runs against an existing key-trusted admin
  foothold. For Cygwin/WSL sshd hardening on Windows nodes (in addition to
  Windows OpenSSH), hardening is operator-manual.

**Garrison-destructive model:**

- Every garrison wipes prior workload state regardless of which investiture
  or operator runs it. Node runtime state belongs to whoever last garrisoned.
- One workload user per node, ever. No conscript / discharge / withdraw /
  vanquish / inventory verbs; garrison subsumes them.
- Admin-side sshd configuration is fenestrate's scope on Windows;
  operator-manual elsewhere.

**Cross-cutting premises:**

- `burn_host_singularity` — host of a node lives only in BURN; BURP reaches
  it by reference, never duplicates.
- `bus_keys_operator_owned` — system never generates or modifies SSH key
  material; operator owns all key administration.

**Tabtarget colophons (14 total; b/c/w shell support, PowerShell deferred):**

- `buw-r[np][lrv]` — BURN/BURP regime config (List/Render/Validate) — 6
- `buw-jpF` — Fenestrate (Windows OpenSSH only; uniform) — 1
- `buw-jpG[bcw]` — Garrison per shell-letter (b=native bash for Linux/Mac;
  c=Cygwin; w=WSL) — 3
- `buw-jwk` — Knock (probe workload reachability) — 1
- `buw-jwc` — Run command file as workload (shell determined by garrison) — 1
- `buw-jws` — Interactive SSH as workload — 1
- `buw-hj0` — Handbook jurisdiction top — 1

**Operational seat (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh` + `bujb_cli.sh`):**

BCG-compliant module housing both fenestrate and garrison implementations,
hardcoding the three shell-letter→`command=` directive mappings (b/c/w)
used by garrison on the workload account, the workload privkey destination
path on remote per shell-letter, the canonical WSL distribution name
(`rbtww-main`), and the Windows OpenSSH `sshd_config` hardening directive
set used by fenestrate. SSH transport mechanics live in code; BURP carries
no shell selection.

**Fenestrate ceremony (Windows OpenSSH only; uniform):**

Two-phase remote-run because `Restart-Service sshd` terminates its own
ssh session.

Phase 1 (over the password-or-key admin SSH session):

1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password`. Steady-state:
   `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE` authenticates by key. First-run: ssh prompts
   on /dev/tty; operator types the Windows admin password once. Fenestrate
   process never sees the password.
2. Idempotently place admin pubkey (derived from `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`
   via `ssh-keygen -y`) in `administrators_authorized_keys` as a bare entry
   (no `command=`; fenestrate is uniform). Performed before any sshd_config
   change so key auth is available for phase 2's reconnect.
3. `icacls` lockdown on `administrators_authorized_keys`.
4. Write hardened directives to `sshd_config`: `PubkeyAuthentication yes`,
   `PasswordAuthentication no`, `PermitEmptyPasswords no`. Idempotent.
5. Verify written state via PowerShell `Get-Content` returning raw bytes,
   then bash-side BCG-compliant matching extracting effective directive
   values. Keeps PowerShell out of parsing (its error semantics are
   unreliable).
6. `sshd -t` validates the config (penultimate atomic op of phase 1; a
   malformed config aborts fenestrate before it could brick running sshd).
7. `Restart-Service sshd` (final atomic op of phase 1; ssh session
   terminates here — bash orchestrator treats the disconnect as the
   expected phase boundary).

Phase 2 (key auth only):

8. Reconnect as `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey` (no password fallback). Failure
   here surfaces a brick — the operator's manual password no longer opens
   a session.
9. Verify post-state: the admin session authenticates by key alone and the
   running sshd serves the hardened configuration.

**Garrison ceremony (per shell-letter; workload-only):**

1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`; key auth
   only (no password fallback). On Windows, presupposes fenestrate has run
   for this investiture. On Linux/Mac, admin trust is operator-managed
   (e.g., `ssh-copy-id` placed the admin pubkey).
2. Destroy existing workload user (if present) via `userdel -r` or platform
   equivalent — removes account + home directory; stray files outside home
   are workload's own concern, not garrison's.
3. Create fresh workload user named `BUJB_workload_user` (unprivileged: no
   sudo, no admin group, ssh-only access).
4. Place workload pubkey in workload's authorized_keys with the
   shell-letter's `command=` directive.
5. Copy workload privkey to remote workload account at the path hardcoded in
   `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` per shell-letter (so workload can authenticate
   outbound to GitHub).
6. Validate round-trip — SSH as workload + exec no-op + verify exit 0.

## Heat Sequence

**Spec foundation:**
- spec-bus0-jurisdiction — BUS0 rewrite reflecting current concept
- introduce-fenestrate-narrow-garrison — split admin trust + sshd harden
  out of garrison into a uniform fenestrate verb

**Implementation:**
- implement-jurisdiction — four-field BURP, `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER`,
  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` module, 14 tabtargets, fenestrate + garrison
  ceremonies, cleanup superseded colophons + handbook entries

**Investigation (independent, runs anytime):**
- localhost-fundus-parallel-saturation diagnosis

**Practice (Windows host, blocks on implementation):**
- WSL distribution install (`rbtww-main` set as default)
- Garrison-WSL exercise
- Garrison-Cygwin exercise
- Docker Desktop WSL integration enable + verify (no native dockerd)

## Deferred (named for future return)

- **PowerShell garrison + dispatch** — `buw-jpGp` tabtarget + the `p`
  shell-letter mapping in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Current heat ships b/c/w
  only.
- **Adopt** — cross-station-user pubkey install via existing privileged trust.
  Today: each station user runs the ceremony fresh from their own machine.
- **User regime** — centralized per-user pubkey for shared admin scenarios.
- **Separate workload→GitHub keypair** — current shape reuses workload SSH
  keypair for outbound; tighten to per-purpose separation if security demands.
- **BURP override fields for non-conventional key layouts** — operators with
  centralized SSH key storage, non-`.pub`-suffix layouts.
- **Localhost garrison/dispatch variants** — deferred; remote nodes only.
- **Git normalization on remote** — JJK has `jjx_plant`; BUK doesn't duplicate.
- **Cygwin / WSL sshd hardening verb** — fenestrate covers Windows OpenSSH
  only. If c/w garrisons ever require their own sshd to be hardened
  programmatically (rather than operator-managed), that is a future
  fenestrate sibling.
- **Native dockerd inside `rbtww-main`** — Docker Desktop and a per-distro
  native daemon cannot safely coexist on the same Windows host: WSL2 distros
  share one network namespace, so two daemons fight for kernel iptables/NAT
  rules. Release-1 ships Docker Desktop only; Desktop's WSL integration
  serves the rbtww-main shells. If isolation needs ever require a native
  daemon, it would be operator-managed exclusive lifecycle (one daemon at
  a time, never both running concurrently).
- **JJK fundus user provisioning under WSL** — fundus is a JJK concept and
  currently deferred; the Windows handbook does not include a fundus phase.

## Standing Notes

- **Fenestrate handles first-time admin trust establishment on Windows.**
  On a fresh node, ssh's `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password` falls
  through to a /dev/tty password prompt; operator types the Windows admin
  password once. Fenestrate then installs the admin pubkey, sets the icacls
  ACL, hardens sshd_config (`PasswordAuthentication no`), restarts sshd,
  and reconnects to verify by key. Subsequent runs use key auth
  automatically. The operator's manual Windows scope reduces to: install
  OpenSSH server, enable the service, set `PasswordAuthentication yes`
  temporarily, allow the firewall port, ensure the admin user has a known
  password.
- **Linux/Mac admin trust is operator-managed.** No fenestrate verb;
  operator places admin pubkey via `ssh-copy-id` (or equivalent), and
  garrison's first admin SSH succeeds by key alone.
- **Two-phase fenestrate is structural, not optional.** `Restart-Service
  sshd` kills its own ssh session, so any post-restart verification must
  happen over a fresh session. Bash orchestrator treats the post-restart
  disconnect as the expected phase boundary, not a failure.
- **Encrypted (passphrase-protected) privkeys not supported.** `ssh-keygen -y`
  prompts interactively; fenestrate and garrison would hang. Operator
  policy: privkeys unencrypted; station-level security covers
  privkey-at-rest protection.
- **Workload pubkey must be pre-registered with GitHub out-of-band.** Operator
  pastes the pubkey into GitHub (deploy key per repo or personal SSH key)
  before garrison runs. Garrison cannot validate this; it is a precondition.
- **WSL distribution is hardcoded to `rbtww-main`** in
  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. If the current default WSL distribution differs,
  garrison-WSL fails fast with a clear error.
- **Docker Desktop is the release-1 container runtime on Windows.** WSL
  shells reach the daemon via Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL integration
  toggle (Settings → Resources → WSL Integration), enabled for `rbtww-main`.
  Cygwin and Windows-side shells reach the same daemon via the default
  Docker context. No separate dockerd inside the WSL distro; only one
  daemon ever runs concurrently.
- **Some `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` values may contain spaces** (e.g., Windows
  admin user `b hyslop`); audit shell quoting at every variable expansion and
  path construction.
- Tailscale provides stable transport (Mac sees `rocket` as `100.x.y.z` /
  tailnet hostname); not adopted as a BUK dependency.
- PowerShell-from-WSL pattern: bash owns control flow, PowerShell is the
  Windows syscall layer; one atomic command per call.
- `command=` in authorized_keys is OpenSSH-standard (used by gitolite, GitHub,
  rsync, borg); shrinks key blast radius rather than expanding it.

# jjezs_pace ₢A-AAv

## Character
Resume after WSG transport rules established; mechanical fix + verification cycle.

## Docket

Remaining work: step 5's `install: invalid user 'bujuw_user'` — body uses
plain `${ztmp}` and `$(mktemp)` which get eaten in transit (something
between cmd.exe and Linux bash via wsl.exe). Prior cycles landed: channel
rewrite (heredoc → args form), obliterate orphan-purge + userdel reorder,
wrapper $LASTEXITCODE init repair, BCG-compliant transport (no $()/pipeline
in module code, no remote temp file). Garrison-w on bujn-winpc clears
obliterate, step 3 (useradd persists in /etc/passwd with bujuw_user-owned
home + skel copied), and step 4 (Windows-side authorized_keys placed).
Step 5 is the lone remaining failure.

Locked decisions:

- Read WSG (Tools/buk/vov_veiled/WSG-WindowsScriptingGuide.md) before
  mount. WSG's body-side escape rule (formerly OQ-4, established by the
  experiment pace) is canonical — apply it to bodies, choosing the layer
  (caller-side per-arg escape, or zbujb_admin_exec doing the escape
  internally) per WSG's recommendation. Future Windows-transport bodies
  follow the same rule.
- Step 5 b|w branch is the only current body with $ references that need
  the escape. Steps 3 and 4 (w branch) have no $ references and worked
  in cycle 3 unmodified — leave alone unless WSG-OQ4 dictates otherwise.
- Diagnostic instrumentation in zbujb_obliterate_windows_namespaces — the
  per-step diag_dump helper and baseline Get-Date probe added during the
  diagnostic cycles — remains in place. Pace-close decision: keep with a
  doc-comment naming it forensic, or prune (remove helper + 11 call
  sites). Operator preference at close.

Done when:
- bash -n green on bujb_jurisdiction.sh
- tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh green
- tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh green at baseline
- tt/buw-jpGw.GarrisonWsl.sh bujn-winpc runs end-to-end through all 6
  steps including step-6 round-trip knock, with workload identity
  bujuw_user
- BUSJGW reads coherently against implemented behavior
- No BURC_WORKLOAD_USER references remain (verified clean in cycle 1;
  preserve)
- Diag_dump instrumentation decision recorded (kept-with-note or pruned)

content.text: Reading WSG to understand the canonical body-side escape rule before assessing approach.

content.content:
1	# WSG — Windows Scripting Guide
2	
3	## Purpose
4	
5	WSG codifies the discipline for embedding remote shell actions (PowerShell or
6	bash) inside ssh sessions to Windows hosts, with full error trapping at every
7	layer. WSG extends BCG's bash discipline into the ssh-to-Windows transport
8	stack — where curia bash code authors a string that traverses ssh → Windows
9	OpenSSH → cmd.exe → child shell → script execution.
10	
11	WSG is empirically derived. Every established rule has a verification probe
12	that demonstrates the failure mode the rule prevents. Open questions are
13	flagged explicitly and resolved by separate experiment paces, not by
14	conjecture.
15	
16	## Core Philosophy
17	
18	**Every layer is a fault domain.** A character that means one thing in curia
19	bash means another in cmd.exe, another in the Windows argv parser, another
20	in PowerShell's `-Command` parser, and another in Linux bash. The transport
21	**transforms** the body at each boundary. Errors at any layer can be
22	silently swallowed if not explicitly trapped.
23	
24	**The cmd.exe layer is invisible from curia.** The user's command is wrapped
25	in `cmd.exe /c <command>` before any other shell sees it. Cmd.exe's quoting
26	rules — `"..."` only; no single-quote support; idiosyncratic `\` handling —
27	bite even bodies that look bash-correct from the curia.
28	
29	**Some object output formatters are lazy.** PowerShell flushes string
30	output (Write-Host, Write-Output of strings) eagerly; some cmdlets'
31	formatter cycles flush only on full pipeline completion, so calling
32	`exit` mid-body (or trailer-side) discards their unformatted output.
33	The set of lazy cmdlets is not universal — `Get-LocalUser` is proven
34	lazy, `Get-Item` is proven eager. Treat the lazy default as the safe
35	assumption (PS-2). String emission always survives.
36	
37	**Trap, don't trust.** Each rule below assumes that any error which can
38	plausibly be silenced WILL be silenced if not explicitly trapped. The bias
39	is toward defensive instrumentation: surface every non-zero exit, every
40	empty stdout, every unflushed buffer, with a forensic temp file naming the
41	boundary that swallowed it.
42	
43	## Transport Stack
44	
45	A typical chain for `zbujb_admin_powershell` or `zbujb_admin_exec`:
46	
47	```
48	1. Local bash (curia)            builds ssh argument string
49	2. ssh client                    forwards command to remote
50	3. Windows OpenSSH sshd          spawns DefaultShell (cmd.exe)
51	4. cmd.exe /c <command>          parses tokens, respects "...", spawns child
52	5. Child shell (one of):
53	   a. powershell.exe -Command "<body>"     runs body in PowerShell
54	   b. wsl.exe ... bash -c "<body>"          forwards to Linux bash in WSL
55	   c. C:/cygwin64/bin/bash --login -c "..." runs Cygwin bash
56	6. Body subprocess(es)           child processes inherit parent's FD 0
57	```
58	
59	Each step has its own quoting model. Each can fail silently.
60	
61	## Established Rules
62	
63	### ❌ PS-1: Trailing `exit $LASTEXITCODE` without LASTEXITCODE init
64	
65	In a fresh `powershell -Command` invocation with no native command run,
66	`$LASTEXITCODE` is `$null`. PowerShell's typed comparison treats `$null -ne 0`
67	as `True`. A trailer like `if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit $LASTEXITCODE }`
68	always fires for cmdlet-only bodies, calling `exit $null` (which exits 0 in
69	practice). The `exit` short-circuits PowerShell's lazy object-formatter
70	pipeline, discarding any cmdlet output buffered by Out-Default that hasn't
71	flushed to stdout yet.
72	
73	```powershell
74	# ❌ Eats Get-LocalUser's table output (returns CRLF only)
75	powershell -Command "Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue;
76	                     if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit $LASTEXITCODE }"
77	
78	# ✅ Initialize so the branch only fires on real native command failures
79	powershell -Command "$LASTEXITCODE = 0;
80	                     Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue;
81	                     if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit $LASTEXITCODE }"
82	```
83	
84	Probe pair (verification):
85	
86	```
87	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> \
88	  "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo'; if (\$LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit \$LASTEXITCODE }\""
89	# stdout → CRLF only
90	
91	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> \
92	  "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"\$LASTEXITCODE = 0; Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo'; if (\$LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit \$LASTEXITCODE }\""
93	# stdout → full LocalUser table
94	```
95	
96	### ❌ PS-2: `exit` mid-body before object formatters flush
97	
98	PowerShell flushes string output eagerly but *some* cmdlet object outputs
99	lazily (Out-Default → Format-Table → render). `exit` aborts the formatter
100	pipeline. Lazy outputs that haven't flushed are discarded. The cmdlets
101	that exhibit this lazy behavior are formatter-cycle-specific: `Get-LocalUser`
102	from `Microsoft.PowerShell.LocalAccounts` is proven lazy (empty stdout
103	when followed by `exit 0`); `Get-Item` from the FileSystem provider is
104	proven eager (table survives `exit 0`). Treat the rule as the safe
105	default: assume any cmdlet output is lazy unless you've checked. See
106	the OQ-6 section of `Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md`
107	for the empirical baseline.
108	
109	```powershell
110	# ❌ Get-LocalUser's table is lost; Get-Date's string survives
111	powershell -Command "Get-Date; Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo'; exit 0"
112	
113	# ✅ Materialize objects to strings before any potential exit
114	powershell -Command "Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo' | Out-String; ..."
115	```
116	
117	If a wrapper provides `$LASTEXITCODE = 0` initialization (PS-1), this rule
118	is moot for happy-path bodies. Failure paths still need string-materialization
119	guards if any cmdlet output must survive the trailer's exit branch.
120	
121	### ❌ SH-1: Script via stdin to bash (heredoc form / `bash -s`)
122	
123	When ssh feeds a script to remote bash via stdin — `bash -s` on the remote
124	with heredoc on the local side, or `echo BODY | bash` chains on the remote —
125	bash reads commands from FD 0. Child processes spawned by that bash inherit
126	FD 0, connected to the script-providing pipe. A child that reads FD 0
127	(notably Windows binaries called via wsl.exe interop) consumes script bytes
128	bash hadn't yet parsed. Bash hits EOF prematurely, exits 0 without errors,
129	and remaining commands silently never execute.
130	
131	This is BCG's stdin-consumption discipline (BCG line 1388) at a different
132	scope.
133	
134	```bash
135	# ❌ Script via heredoc/stdin; net.exe (or any FD-0 reader) eats bytes
136	ssh ... "wsl.exe ... bash -s" <<SCRIPT
137	set -euo pipefail
138	net.exe user 'foo' /add ... > /dev/null
139	useradd 'foo'                       # may silently never run
140	SCRIPT
141	
142	# ✅ Body as bash -c argument; bash reads body from -c, not FD 0
143	ssh ... "wsl.exe ... bash -c \"set -euo pipefail; net.exe ...; useradd 'foo'\""
144	```
145	
146	Verification: cycle-1 vs cycle-3 of correct-wsl-user-model (`₢A-AAv`).
147	Heredoc form silently dropped useradd; args form propagated correctly,
148	proven by `getent passwd bujuw_user` returning the entry post-step-3.
149	
150	### ❌ SH-2: Multi-line bodies through cmd.exe
151	
152	Cmd.exe is line-oriented. Newlines in `"..."` quoted args are unreliable
153	across the cmd.exe → child-binary boundary. Use `;` as the bash command
154	separator and ship the body as one line.
155	
156	```bash
157	# ❌ Newlines in body; cmd.exe may fragment
158	ssh ... "wsl.exe ... bash -c \"
159	set -e
160	useradd foo
161	\""
162	
163	# ✅ Single-line body with ; separators
164	ssh ... "wsl.exe ... bash -c \"set -e;useradd foo\""
165	```
166	
167	### ✅ SH-3: Outer `"..."` with `\"` for embedded double-quotes
168	
169	The Windows argv parser (used by wsl.exe and most Windows binaries) honors
170	`\"` inside `"..."` as an escape for literal `"`. This survives cmd.exe and
171	reaches Linux bash as `"`. Cmd.exe itself does NOT process `\"`; it passes
172	the bytes to the child whose argv parser handles the escape.
173	
174	```bash
175	# ✅ Embedded " in body
176	ssh ... "wsl.exe ... bash -c \"trap 'rm -f \\\"\$path\\\"' EXIT\""
177	```
178	
179	Probe: `bash -c "echo SAW: \"hello\""` → stdout `SAW: hello` (quotes
180	stripped by bash's own parsing of the unescaped `"`).
181	
182	### ✅ PS-3: PowerShell single-quoted strings survive nesting
183	
184	Inside the outer `"..."` of `powershell -Command "..."`, single-quoted
185	strings (`'Stop'`, `'username'`) pass through cleanly. Cmd.exe does not
186	honor single quotes for arg-grouping but treats them as literal characters;
187	PowerShell parses its body and recognizes `'...'` as PS string literals.
188	
189	```bash
190	# ✅ Single-quoted PS literals inside outer "..."
191	ssh ... "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"Get-LocalUser -Name 'bujuw_user'\""
192	```
193	
194	This is why `zbujb_admin_powershell` consistently uses single quotes for PS
195	string literals — they nest reliably through every layer.
196	
197	### ✅ PS-4: PS-2's lazy-flush behavior is transport-agnostic
198	
199	The PS-2 lazy-flush is a property of the PowerShell body, not of how
200	PowerShell was launched. The same `Get-LocalUser; exit 0` body emits
201	empty stdout via direct cmd.exe→powershell, cygwin→powershell.exe, and
202	wsl.exe→bash→powershell.exe. The fix (Out-String materialization, or
203	PS-1's `$LASTEXITCODE = 0` so the trailer doesn't fire) is also
204	transport-agnostic. See `Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md`
205	§OQ-6.
206	
207	### ❌ SH-4: cmd.exe does NOT process `$()` or `$var`
208	
209	Verified by direct probe: `cmd.exe /c echo "SHELL=$0 X=$(uname)"` emits the
210	literal string `"SHELL=$0 X=$(uname)"` with no expansion. Cmd.exe's
211	variable syntax is `%var%` and it has no command substitution. Therefore
212	the outer cmd.exe layer is NOT the layer that processes shell variable
213	expansion or command substitution in transit.
214	
215	This rule is structural; the corollary is OQ-1 (something else does eat `$`
216	in some chains).
217	
218	### ❌ SH-5: BCG bans extend across the transport boundary
219	
220	The bash code we author at the curia AND the bash code we author for remote
221	execution are both subject to BCG. In particular:
222	
223	- **No heredocs anywhere.** The transport must not use `bash -s` with
224	  heredoc input (SH-1 covers the failure mode); module callers must not
225	  build remote bodies via heredoc.
226	- **No pipelines inside `$()`.** This applies to module code (e.g. the
227	  ill-fated `z_b64=$(printf ... | base64 | tr -d ...)` transport) and to
228	  bodies authored for remote execution.
229	- **No unguarded `$()`.** Remote bodies that use command substitution must
230	  guard against silent failure the same way curia code does.
231	
232	The `mktemp` exception in BCG line 1473 (introspection allowlist) carries
233	through; remote bodies may use `$(mktemp)` for temp file creation. Top-level
234	pipelines (not inside `$()`) are not banned by BCG and are permitted in
235	remote bodies (see e.g. step-5's `openssl enc -base64 -d`).
236	
237	### ❌ SH-6: wsl.exe substitutes `$name` and `${name}` in argv
238	
239	The wsl.exe argv-to-Linux-invocation processor performs `$name` and
240	`${name}` substitution against its own (Linux-side, root-shell startup)
241	environment before bash receives the body. Names present in that
242	environment substitute to their values; undefined names substitute to
243	empty. **Cygwin bash does not exhibit this behavior. cmd.exe alone (per
244	SH-4) also does not.** `$(...)` command substitution is unaffected (the
245	`(` after `$` does not match the `$name`/`${name}` token shape).
246	
247	Per-letter escape table:
248	
249	| Letter | Path                                       | `$name`     | `${name}`     | `$(...)` |
250	|--------|--------------------------------------------|-------------|---------------|----------|
251	| b      | direct ssh to Linux/Mac                    | none        | none          | none     |
252	| c      | cmd.exe → cygwin bash                      | none        | none          | none     |
253	| w      | cmd.exe → wsl.exe → bash                   | `\$name`    | `\${name}`    | none     |
254	
255	Probe pair (verification, w-letter):
256	
257	```
258	# ❌ Fails on w-letter — wsl.exe substitutes $ztmp to empty before bash runs
259	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "ztmp=HELLO; echo $ztmp"'
260	# stdout: (empty)
261	
262	# ✅ \$ defers expansion to bash
263	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "ztmp=HELLO; echo \$ztmp"'
264	# stdout: HELLO
265	```
266	
267	See `Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md` §OQ-1, §OQ-2,
268	§OQ-4.
269	
270	### ✅ SH-7: Exit-code propagation is reliable across transports
271	
272	Non-zero exit codes propagate cleanly through every tested transport:
273	cmd.exe direct, wsl.exe → bash, cygwin bash, PowerShell, and native
274	Windows binaries called via wsl.exe interop. `set -e` aborts the script
275	on first failure and the failing exit reaches the curia unchanged.
276	Bodies that need to fail-loud should use `set -e` or explicit `exit N`.
277	
278	Probe (w-letter, set -e + false):
279	
280	```
281	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "set -e; false; echo UNREACHED"'; echo "EXIT=$?"
282	# (no UNREACHED in stdout)
283	# EXIT=1
284	```
285	
286	See `Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md` §OQ-5.
287	
288	### ✅ SH-8: Multi-line bodies via file feed (when SH-2 doesn't fit)
289	
290	When a body materially exceeds what `;`-join (SH-2) can express, the
291	canonical pattern is two-phase: phase 1 ships the body as a remote file
292	via PowerShell `Set-Content` or `[System.IO.File]::WriteAllText`; phase 2
293	runs `wsl.exe ... bash /path/to/file` (or `cygwin bash --login -c "bash
294	/path/to/file"`). Side benefit: bypasses wsl.exe's argv `$`-substitution
295	(SH-6) since the script body comes from disk, not argv.
296	
297	Caveats:
298	- PS `Set-Content` writes CRLF. Either use an LF-explicit writer:
299	
300	  ```powershell
301	  [System.IO.File]::WriteAllText($path, ($lines -join "`n"))
302	  ```
303	
304	  or normalize CRLF→LF into a *second* file before exec.
305	- Do NOT pipe the file into `bash` via stdin; that reintroduces SH-1's
306	  stdin-consumption hazard. Use `bash /path/to/file`, not `bash <file`.
307	- The two-phase approach is not atomic; the caller flow must clean up
308	  the remote temp file.
309	
310	Default discipline remains SH-2; SH-8 applies only when bodies don't fit
311	one line. See `Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md`
312	§OQ-7.
313	
314	## Wrapper Discipline
315	
316	### PowerShell wrapper (proven shape)
317	
318	Mirrors `zbujb_admin_powershell` after the LASTEXITCODE-init repair:
319	
320	```bash
321	ssh ... "${USER}@${HOST}" \
322	    "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"\$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'; \$env:WSL_UTF8 = 1; \$LASTEXITCODE = 0; ${z_body}; if (\$LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit \$LASTEXITCODE }\""
323	```
324	
325	Discipline:
326	- `\$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'` — PS errors terminate
327	- `\$env:WSL_UTF8 = 1` — wsl.exe (when called from PS body) emits UTF-8
328	- `\$LASTEXITCODE = 0` — initialize to defeat the null trap (PS-1)
329	- Trailing `if ... exit ...` — propagate native command exit codes
330	- Body uses single quotes for PS literals (PS-3)
331	- Body avoids object-emitting cmdlets immediately before exit, or pipes
332	  them through Out-String first (PS-2)
333	
334	### Bash-via-wsl.exe wrapper (proven shape, w-letter)
335	
336	Mirrors `zbujb_admin_exec w` after the cycle-3 BCG-clean repair. Body is
337	`;`-joined for one-line transit (SH-2), `"` escaped to `\"` (SH-3), and
338	all `$name`/`${name}` references escaped to `\$name`/`\${name}` for
339	deferred bash-side expansion (SH-6). `$(...)` command substitution does
340	not need escape (SH-6).
341	
342	```bash
343	ssh ... "${USER}@${HOST}" \
344	    "wsl.exe --distribution ${DIST} --user root bash -c \"${z_body}\""
345	```
346	
347	Body authoring discipline:
348	- Statements joined with `;` (SH-2)
349	- Embedded `"` → `\"` (SH-3)
350	- `$name` → `\$name`; `${name}` → `\${name}` (SH-6)
351	- `$(...)` left as-is (SH-6)
352	- No heredocs, no `bash -s`, no inner pipe-then-bash (SH-1, SH-5)
353	- No newlines in body (SH-2); use SH-8 if the body genuinely won't fit
354	
355	### Bash-via-cygwin wrapper (proven shape, c-letter)
356	
357	Cygwin's path is simpler. cmd.exe → C:/cygwin64/bin/bash.exe. No wsl.exe,
358	no Windows-side `$` substitution.
359	
360	```bash
361	ssh ... "${USER}@${HOST}" \
362	    "C:/cygwin64/bin/bash --login -c \"${z_body}\""
363	```
364	
365	Body authoring discipline:
366	- Statements joined with `;` (SH-2)
367	- Embedded `"` → `\"` (SH-3)
368	- `$name`, `${name}`, `$(...)` all unescaped — bash sees them directly (SH-6)
369	- No heredocs, no `bash -s` (SH-1, SH-5)
370	
371	## Deferred — out of release-1 scope
372	
373	### b-letter (Linux/Mac native ssh) verification
374	
375	No Linux or Mac BURN profile is registered in the release-1 matrix; only
376	`bujn-winpc` (`BURN_PLATFORM=bubep_windows`). The b-letter path bypasses
377	every Windows-specific layer (no cmd.exe DefaultShell, no wsl.exe argv
378	substitution, no Windows argv parser); standard BCG body discipline
379	applies. If a Linux/Mac BURN profile is added, run the OQ-1 probes from
380	`Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md` (probes 1A, 1G,
381	1J, 1P) substituting plain bash for wsl.exe / cygwin to confirm absence
382	of Windows-layer quirks. See §OQ-3 of that memo.
383	
384	## Empirical Record
385	
386	Probe matrices, raw transcripts, and the bisection narratives that
387	generated the rules in this document are filed under `Memos/`. WSG cites
388	those memos but does not duplicate their contents.
389	
390	Active references:
391	
392	- `Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md` — initial OQ-1
393	  through OQ-7 resolution matrix run against `bujn-winpc` (pace `₢A-AA0`).
394	
395	### Convention for future Windows-transport experiments
396	
397	When a new open question surfaces around the Windows transport stack
398	(wsl.exe, cygwin, cmd.exe, OpenSSH-Win32, PowerShell), file the probe
399	matrix and resolution at:
400	
401	```
402	Memos/memo-YYYYMMDD-windows-transport-{topic}.md
403	```
404	
405	Cite the memo from WSG once findings stabilize into rules. WSG retains
406	the distilled rules and one-line probe pairs; memos retain the full
407	empirical record.
408	
409	## Verification Probe Template
410	
411	Every WSG rule has a probe that demonstrates the failure mode and the
412	fix. Standard form:
413	
414	```
415	# Probe N: <claim>
416	# Pre-state: <relevant remote state>
417	
418	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> "<failing form>"
419	# stdout: <observed>
420	# exit:   <observed>
421	
422	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> "<repaired form>"
423	# stdout: <observed>
424	# exit:   <observed>
425	```
426	
427	Probes are read-only and idempotent where possible. Probes that mutate
428	remote state (creating users, files) must declare cleanup or rely on a
429	subsequent obliterate cycle.

435	| WSG | Windows Scripting Guide (this document) |

437	| Curia | The local invoking machine (per JJK terminology) |
